review_text: Not such a great way to fly. I booked an air ticket and the flight departure timing was brought forward by 7 hours. We had the option to accept, reschedule or cancel to get a refund. I chose the third option but was put thru multiple individuals who do not seem to know what they are doing (each contradicting the other) but eventually, I received an email to say the refund was being processed and it would take 6 weeks. Well, it was the 6th week yesterday and I wrote in to enquire, only to be told they will be processing it now. Today I received another email (a proper refund notice this time) to say it will be another 6 weeks for the refund to come thru.
issue: Flight departure time was moved up by 7 hours, causing inconvenience.
fixes: Deploy an automated schedule‑change notification workflow in your CRM (e.g., Salesforce Service Cloud) that triggers a personalized email/SMS within 15 minutes of any flight‑time adjustment, including the new departure date/time and a “rebook now” link to available alternative flights. | - Integrate a real‑time disruption monitoring dashboard with FlightAware APIs into the airline’s operations center so dispatchers can instantly see affected itineraries and automatically reassign seats on other flights when a 7‑hour shift occurs. | - Launch a self‑service rebooking portal built on Amadeus Altéa, allowing passengers to modify bookings or request compensation without contacting call centers, with a 24‑hour SLA for processing requests. | - Implement a rule‑based compensation engine in Sabre Red Workspace that automatically issues travel vouchers or mileage credits for flights whose departure times are changed by more than 6 hours, ensuring consistent treatment across all routes. | - Establish a dedicated “Schedule Change Response Team” staffed by two agents per shift, tracked via a Zendesk ticket queue with a target first‑response time of ≤5 minutes, and log each case in a Power BI KPI report to monitor adherence weekly.
rationale: The response effectively addresses the specific issue of a flight departure time change, providing actionable and relevant recommendations. It specifies concrete tools such as Salesforce Service Cloud, FlightAware APIs, Amadeus Altéa, Sabre Red Workspace, Zendesk ticket queues, and Power BI KPI reports, which are used to implement real-world solutions for scheduling changes and rebooking processes. The response also suggests feasible actions such as automated notifications, real-time monitoring, a self-service portal, an automated compensation engine, and the establishment of a dedicated team. These recommendations align well with the customer's needs and demonstrate an understanding of the operational challenges faced by airlines. Furthermore, the response is succinct, directly addressing the issue without any unnecessary elaboration. Thus, it meets the criteria outlined in the score rubric for both relevance and actionability, while also displaying high levels of concreteness and feasibility.

review_text: The check-in process at JFK was the worst check-in experience I've experienced. I was waiting for check-in for over 2 hours. The line just didn't move at all. The agent was extremely inefficient. I was one of the last customers to check in and it was about 30 minutes before the departure time. The counter agents was escorting a group of last-to-check-in customers to the security checkpoint so they could skip the line (we were late) and somehow I was the only one who left in the security line on my own. The service on board was terrible despite the high-regarded in-flight service that Singapore Airlines is usually known for. The service was so slow for a flight with only 160 customers.  I sat at one of the single seat toward the end of the aircraft and the steward kept bumping into me like I was sitting on the aisle. He didn't even apologize. My food was OK at best. The worst part was probably the lavatories. Truly disgusting, trash was everywhere on the floor.
issue: Check-in wait time exceeded two hours and the line moved very slowly, with inefficient agents.
fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based queue management platform (e.g., QLess) integrated with the property’s POS to display real‑time wait estimates on digital signage and push SMS alerts, reducing perceived wait time by at least 15 %. | - Implement a workforce‑management solution such as Kronos Workforce Central to forecast peak check‑in windows based on historical arrival patterns and automatically schedule additional agents 30 minutes before predicted spikes. | - Launch a mobile pre‑check‑in app that captures guest ID and payment details via QR code scanning, then route those records directly into the PMS so agents only need to confirm arrival, cutting manual data entry time by up to 40 %. | - Install self‑service kiosks equipped with barcode scanners and a step‑by‑step workflow from NCR SelfServe, allowing guests to complete registration independently while freeing human agents to handle complex cases. | - Conduct a weekly 20‑minute “process sprint” using Miro boards to map bottlenecks, assign owners, and track KPIs (average wait per guest, agent idle time), with a target of ≤10 % variance in wait times month‑over‑month.

review_text: Siem Reap to Kolkata via Singapore. We chose Singapore as we had heard lots of wonderful things about the airline. Our experience was worse than any bucket-shop airlines and we thought Singapore was a world standard airline. On the Siem Reap to Singapore Route my seat had a broken table which I was informed only on the way to the boarding the flight when I was suddenly being pressurised by their ground staff. Although I was waiting at the airport near the gate for over an hour none of your ground staff bothered to discuss this matter with me.  Furthermore the table was only secured with sticky tape which kept falling on my lap and on a couple of occasions fell on my watch! We then received further shoddy service from ground staff at Singapore on the Kolkata flight. Having paid USD 700+ for our one-way ticket from REP to CCU I was shocked to be downgraded to an airline with filthy seats, no TV, no leg space and in general badly in need of an uplift. To cap it all the cabin staff offered me gifts worth SGD 75 on the plane. This offer got progressively downgraded to a few miles on their airline.
issue: Seat had a broken table that was only disclosed at boarding and was poorly secured with sticky tape, causing it to fall on the passenger’s lap and watch
fixes: Deploy a digital “Seat‑Fit Inspection” checklist in the airline’s mobile inspection app (e.g., iAuditor) that crew must complete before every boarding; the checklist requires photo evidence of the table latch and adhesive strength, and logs any deviations for immediate supervisor review. | - Integrate a real‑time seat‑condition dashboard into the cabin crew’s handheld device (e.g., iPad with ServiceNow Field Service Management) that flags any “table‑securement = not met” alerts and auto‑generates a corrective work order for re‑installation within 15 minutes of discovery. | - Install RFID‑enabled seat‑table modules linked to the aircraft’s maintenance management system (e.g., AMOS) so that any module flagged out‑of‑alignment triggers an automatic preventive‑maintenance task before the next flight cycle. | - Publish a standardized SOP video series on the crew’s LMS (e.g., SAP SuccessFactors Learning) that demonstrates proper table latching procedures and adhesive application, with quarterly competency quizzes scored ≥90 % before crew are cleared for duty.
rationale: The response includes all required elements and is formatted in a way that is clear and concise. It presents specific recommendations with concrete tools and processes, such as using the iAuditor app for seat inspections, ServiceNow Field Service Management for real-time monitoring, and SAP SuccessFactors Learning for standardizing procedures. This demonstrates a high degree of relevance and actionability that aligns well with the rubric criteria. Furthermore, each recommendation is clearly linked to an existing tool or process, indicating feasibility in terms of cost, effort, and time to implement. The response does not default to a score of 4 but justifies the high score by explicitly mentioning all the aspects of the rubric and providing detailed rationale for why these recommendations are superior. So the overall score is 92.

review_text: We were given a flight with Scoot from Bangkok to Singapore, and then with Singapore to Auckland. The flight with Scoot was perfectly acceptable but what was not was that they decided that we must collect our boarding pass in Singapore which might have been OK but they they never told us where to collect them from, so we we spent ages trying to find the location and only just made it to the gate in time. The previous leg from Milan to Hanoi involved a Scoot flight and we received all our passes in Milan. On the flight when meals were served the crew just appeared at the seat with a tray whilst we were dozing and expected us to put out the table. As we had ordered book the cook, the main meal service took a while and we had finished our meal before drinks were offered. Premium economy food was no better than Economy fare and considering the cost of the flight a little more thought could have gone into the meal.
issue: Passengers were not informed where to collect their boarding passes in Singapore, causing confusion and a rushed arrival at the gate.
fixes: Deploy digital signage linked to the airline’s real‑time flight information API (e.g., FlightAware) at all check‑in zones in Singapore, displaying “Boarding Pass Collection – Gate X” with QR codes that passengers can scan for instant directions. | - Integrate the airport’s Wayfinding app (or a custom mobile push notification via Firebase) so that upon ticket issuance, a pop‑up appears on passengers’ phones with step‑by‑step maps and exact location of the designated pass‑collection counter. | - Install self‑service kiosks equipped with NFC readers that automatically print boarding passes and push a SMS/WhatsApp message containing the collection point, ensuring every passenger receives the information before reaching security. | - Add a mandatory pre‑departure checklist item in the crew’s electronic cabin management system (e.g., SITA Cabin) that logs “passenger notified of pass‑collection zone” and triggers a supervisor alert if the task is missed, enabling immediate corrective action.
rationale: The response is perfectly aligned with the task requirements. Each recommendation clearly targets the issue of service quality related to boarding pass collection in Singapore and offers actionable steps tied to specific tools and processes. The recommendations are concrete and directly address the customer's exact problem. They also include an element of feasibility, which is considered when assessing their effectiveness. However, while all recommendations are well-structured and provide clear steps for implementation, some may require moderate effort in terms of cross-department coordination or software integration, which is why the overall score is not a perfect 5.

review_text: I embarked on a journey with high hopes and anticipation, having secured a connecting flight ticket from Penang to Singapore, and then onwards to Jakarta. Opting for Singapore Airlines, renowned for its outstanding reputation in both air travel and service, I anticipated nothing less than a seamless experience. The cost of this connecting flight amounted to MYR 1601, roughly USD 335, a price I was more than willing to pay for quality service. My journey commenced with a minor delay in takeoff from Penang. The flight proceeded smoothly, accompanied by a light snack comprising a slice of pizza and a beverage of choice. As we approached Changi Airport, the situation took an unexpected turn. The captain announced a delay in landing, estimating an additional 15 to 20 minutes before touchdown. A sense of unease crept over me as my connecting flight departure time loomed closer. Despite my concerns, I remained optimistic, trusting in the efficiency of Singapore Airlines to navigate any challenges seamlessly. Upon landing at 12:05 pm and disembarking at 12:18 pm, I was met with a startling realization. Rather than a smooth transition to my connecting flight, I was handed a new ticket, rescheduled for departure at 4:10 pm, accompanied by a SGD 15 food voucher for Burger King and Coffee Bean.  I sought clarity from the attendant, only to be met with dismissiveness. The absence of any acknowledgment or attempt at redress left me deeply disappointed. The disregard for my time and the indifferent handling of the situation underscored a gap in the airline's commitment to customer service. In retrospect, my expectation for exemplary hospitality and proactive problem-solving from a reputed airline like Singapore Airlines was regrettably unmet. As I reflect on this experience, I am reminded of the importance of genuine care and consideration in customer service, particularly in moments of inconvenience or distress.
issue: The landing delay caused a missed connecting flight and forced a rebooking to a much later departure, disrupting the travel itinerary.
fixes: Deploy a real‑time disruption dashboard in Amadeus Altéa (or Sabre AirVision) that automatically flags any landing delay > 15 minutes and triggers an immediate “hold‑the‑connection” alert to the ground operations team via Slack. | - Integrate Twilio SMS/WhatsApp notifications into the airline’s mobile app so passengers receive a personalized message with alternative gate information and a one‑click rebooking link within 5 minutes of the delay. | - Implement a predictive analytics model in Azure Machine Learning that uses historical weather and aircraft performance data to generate pre‑emptive buffer times for high‑traffic routes; adjust schedule margins accordingly before the flight is published. | - Launch a dedicated “Disruption Recovery” queue in Zendesk with a 20‑minute SLA, staffed by cross‑trained agents who use the same rebooking engine as the original booking to offer passengers at least two viable rebooking options within the hour.
rationale: The candidate response is comprehensive and directly addresses the customer's issue. It presents well-defined recommendations that are actionable and tied to specific tools such as Amadeus Altéa, Sabre AirVision, Slack, Twilio, Azure Machine Learning, Zendesk, and personalized mobile app notifications. The response demonstrates a high level of feasibility by suggesting implementations that can be rolled out quickly and cost-effectively, while also ensuring customer satisfaction through proactive communication and efficient rebooking options. However, the response could have been slightly more concise to achieve a score closer to 70 or 100, as some recommendations were expressed in sentences that exceeded the ideal length of 1-2 sentences.

review_text: The flight was booked at a time when the Covid situation was still looking better and the chance to be be able to leave Changi airport during a long layover looked good. That face with Singapore Airlines flexibility claim of change of flights on their website, made me booking a flight with a 16h layover. Unfortunately, things were not looking good about a month ago, and I tried to change my flights to a short layover. I tried to contact SQ via its change of flight form for Covid related reasons through their website. 5 requests over the span of a month remain unanswered till today, complaint emails were also not replied to. I also tried to contact SQ through their callcenter, was placed in a loop for hours. After getting through after days of trying, I was told that there were seats available for the same booking class and I would be informed once the the ticket would be re-issued. So far so good. An hour later I was called, saying that this „special fare ticket“ can only be changed if if I pay this and that in addition, which ended up to a ridiculous amount, and I declined. So I was back to square and had to endure a 16h stay at Changi airport. SQ does not honor their claim of ticket flexibility. Other carrier I flew during the pandemic were willing honor their flexibility claims. The flights themselves were rather unremarkable, not as polished as Qatar, with no dining on demand offered. Food was average to say the least. No touchscreen TV and average seat on the really noisy 777ER on the long flight. Overall due to the non existing customer service on the ground, not recommended
issue: Customer service was unresponsive; multiple contact attempts via website, email, and call center received no reply or long hold times.
fixes: Deploy a ticketing system (e.g., Zendesk) integrated with the website’s “Contact Us” form and auto‑reply email trigger so every inquiry creates a trackable ticket instantly and generates an acknowledgment within 2 minutes. | - Implement a real‑time queue dashboard in Talkdesk that displays live wait‑times and agent availability, setting an SLA of ≤ 2 minutes average answer time and automatically routing calls to a backup queue when thresholds are breached. | - Create a dedicated “Rapid Response Team” with a rotating shift schedule managed through WhenIWork, assigning two agents per shift to handle escalated tickets flagged by the ticketing system within 15 minutes of creation. | - Send automated follow‑up emails via Mailchimp at 24‑hour intervals for any ticket remaining unresolved, including a clickable “Need help now?” button that routes the case back to the same agent’s queue for immediate resolution.
rationale: The response effectively provides actionable and specific recommendations that are relevant to the customer issue. Each recommendation includes a concrete tool or system that can be implemented immediately. The suggestions are also feasible as they require minimal effort and resources, which makes them easily adoptable without significant cost or disruption.

review_text: The flight was booked at a time when the Covid situation was still looking better and the chance to be be able to leave Changi airport during a long layover looked good. That face with Singapore Airlines flexibility claim of change of flights on their website, made me booking a flight with a 16h layover. Unfortunately, things were not looking good about a month ago, and I tried to change my flights to a short layover. I tried to contact SQ via its change of flight form for Covid related reasons through their website. 5 requests over the span of a month remain unanswered till today, complaint emails were also not replied to. I also tried to contact SQ through their callcenter, was placed in a loop for hours. After getting through after days of trying, I was told that there were seats available for the same booking class and I would be informed once the the ticket would be re-issued. So far so good. An hour later I was called, saying that this „special fare ticket“ can only be changed if if I pay this and that in addition, which ended up to a ridiculous amount, and I declined. So I was back to square and had to endure a 16h stay at Changi airport. SQ does not honor their claim of ticket flexibility. Other carrier I flew during the pandemic were willing honor their flexibility claims. The flights themselves were rather unremarkable, not as polished as Qatar, with no dining on demand offered. Food was average to say the least. No touchscreen TV and average seat on the really noisy 777ER on the long flight. Overall due to the non existing customer service on the ground, not recommended
issue: Lack of dining on demand and average-quality food during the flight.
fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based catering management platform (e.g., CaterTrax) integrated with the airline’s reservation system so passengers can pre‑order meals up to 24 hours before departure and receive real‑time status updates via the airline’s mobile app. | - Install IoT temperature monitoring sensors in all galley refrigerators and cargo holds; configure them to trigger alerts when temperatures deviate >±2 °C from set points, ensuring food stays within optimal freshness windows. | - Introduce a “Fresh‑Cooked” menu option by partnering with local restaurants and using a kitchen‑management dashboard (e.g., Kitchen United) to schedule weekly delivery of fresh ingredients and track preparation times per flight. | - Launch a post‑flight micro‑survey in the airline’s CRM (Salesforce Service Cloud) asking passengers to rate meal quality on a 1‑5 scale; automatically route scores ≤ 3 to a dedicated catering QA team for immediate corrective action.

review_text: Check-in was fast, because I could use the business-class check-in counters as a PPS-member. Otherwise endless queues. Boarding was slow because the flight was full and I boarded late with the crowds. No recognition as a Solitaire-PPS-member on board as before. Cabin crew busy with 'sorting' the passengers and their cabin-luggage to not miss our slot. Seat in a 3-3-3 configuration very narrow, seat-pitch ok (for me being 1.7m only) sufficient, however. Left channel of my speakers was not working, even after exchanging them. Internet not working on my phone, either. No help given to connect, as on earlier flights, instead 'proof' was given to me by a flight attendant that Internet was working on her iphone. Not really helpful. No reading materials. Very basic and little food-options offered in a tiny cardboard-box, 'designed for a reason' (whatever this reason may be). Very inattentive inflight-cabin-crew: As for the after dinner drink I was ignored by the them while my two seat-members got served their drinks of choice. Minimum seat-recline, similar to those in budget-airlines. No soap in washroom. No disinfectant-kits offered as advertised, I got one after request only. Wearing a mask, which is 'mandatory' was not re-enforced, a man in the middle seat was wearing his mask under his chin - no one of the crew cared. Overall the crew seemed to be overworked and de-motivated. The typical glass of champagne offered to me as a PPS-member when I raised my concerns did not really help, it just would have been good enough if the cabin-crew would have fulfilled their duties. Disembarking was fast and swift as well as delivery of my checked-in baggage.
issue: Boarding was slow due to a full flight and crew having to sort passengers and luggage
fixes: Deploy RFID‑enabled boarding passes linked to a real‑time queue‑management dashboard (e.g., QLess) so crew see passenger arrival times on tablets and automatically call groups when space becomes available. | - Integrate the airline’s reservation system with an automated baggage‑sorting workflow in the Amadeus Altéa Baggage Handling module, routing excess bags to off‑site storage before gate closure. | - Use a predictive‑analytics model built in Azure Machine Learning to forecast peak boarding windows 15 minutes ahead and trigger pre‑boarding announcements via airport PA systems and mobile push alerts. | - Implement a “fast‑lane” boarding protocol using a handheld barcode scanner (e.g., Zebra DS3600) at the door that scans a QR code printed on priority tickets, cutting average boarding time by up to 40 seconds per passenger.

issue: No soap was provided in the washroom
fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based inventory management system (e.g., **Zoho Inventory**) with automatic reorder alerts set to trigger when soap stock falls below a 7‑day buffer, ensuring real‑time replenishment. | - Install RFID‑enabled soap dispensers linked to the building’s IoT dashboard so any dispenser drop‑below‑threshold is flagged instantly and staff receive a push notification via **Microsoft Teams** for immediate refill. | - Create a weekly “Washroom Hygiene Checklist” in **Google Forms**, assigning it to the facilities team; if any item (soap availability) remains unchecked after 24 hours, an automated email reminder is sent through **Outlook Flow**. | - Contract a local vendor to deliver pre‑packed soap kits on a fixed schedule using a **Kanban board** in **Jira**, with a KPI of 95 % on‑time delivery tracked and reviewed monthly.
rationale: The recommendations are largely on-topic and actionable. They provide a series of specific measures to improve soap availability in the washroom. The use of concrete tools such as Zoho Inventory, Microsoft Teams, Google Forms, Outlook Flow, and Jira, as well as mentioning RFID technology and IoT dashboard, shows a strong understanding of practical solutions to the problem at hand. However, while the content is largely appropriate and feasible, it does not completely target the root cause of the specific customer issue (lack of soap availability) as the recommendations seem more focused on inventory management rather than addressing the core issue directly.
